content:
Hi everyone, :wave:

I'm currently working on a tooling project aimed at translating Linux kernel C drivers into sound, reviewable Rust. My main focus is on keeping the driver code free of unsafe, confining unsafe operations strictly to abstraction boundaries, and using automated checks to drive the migration.

My goal isn't just to "rewrite C drivers for its own sake", but to figure out what a sound, subsystem-maintainable migration workflow actually looks like in practice.

As an initial experiment, I sent an RFC for an nlmon reference driver to exercise some networking abstractions. However, the feedback from netdev was (understandably) that rewriting an existing in-tree C driver isn't a useful direction unless the subsystem explicitly asks for a Rust reference. (Context here: [Link to your patch thread])

That makes total sense, but it leaves me looking for a better target to validate the tooling and get meaningful upstream review on the generated abstractions.

Does anyone have recommendations for a good target? Ideally, I'm looking for something that is:

  • Not an existing in-tree C driver.

  • Small to moderate in scope (preferably a virtual device or testable without special hardware).

  • Has public C source available (if out-of-tree).

  • Likely to exercise reusable abstractions rather than one-off vendor glue.

I would be super grateful for any guidance on a subsystem that might welcome a small pilot driver, a specific out-of-tree missing driver, or even if you think I should validate this tooling in a completely different way!

For context, my experimental nlmon reference branch is here: (https://github.com/lwz23/rust-for-linux-dev/tree/rfc/nlmon-reference-driver-v1) (I'm keeping the tooling separate from the kernel branch to keep the focus on the generated kernel-facing code).

Thanks in advance!

Wenzhao

If I understand correctly, this is about finding maintainers that may want to invest some time to review AI generated code in order to improve the tooling that does the C to Rust translation?

Or do you mean something else by tooling?

Is the tooling open source?

Hi Miguel, thanks for reaching out! You've captured the core of it, but I want to clarify a few important nuances to address your questions.

1. Regarding "AI-generated code" and the tooling: Yes, the workflow does utilize LLM components under the hood for the C-to-Rust semantic translation. However, it is not a raw code generator. It is a strictly structured pipeline designed to enforce safety boundaries (e.g., ensuring #![forbid(unsafe_code)] in the driver logic).

I want to emphasize that our goal is never to dump raw AI output onto the mailing list or treat maintainers as our debuggers. We heavily manually audit, locally test, and verify the generated code before submitting anything. What we are seeking from the community is not "finding AI bugs," but rather architectural review: Are the Rust abstractions used idiomatically? Is this the right way to model the kernel APIs?

2. The target (In-tree vs Out-of-tree): Exactly as you noted, the nlmon feedback taught us that unsolicited rewrites of working in-tree C drivers are generally not welcome. Therefore, we are looking for:

  • An in-tree module where the maintainer actively wants a Rust reference to exercise their new abstractions.

  • OR, an out-of-tree/missing driver that we could implement in Rust and help upstream.

3. Is the tooling open source? The tooling is part of an ongoing academic research project focused on sound systems migration. It is currently in the active prototyping phase and not yet open source. However, we fully intend to open-source the toolchain alongside our research publication. Right now, we want to contribute useful, well-tested driver code to RFL to ensure our methodology aligns with real upstream standards.

It is a strictly structured pipeline designed to enforce safety boundaries

Hmm... Not sure if I follow this. Adding a few static checks would still mean it is tooling-generated, no?

We heavily manually audit, locally test, and verify the generated code before submitting anything.

That is definitely better, but it all depends on the degree of review that has been done etc. It will likely heavily depend on the maintainer you need to talk to, as well as the quality of the patches submitted. In other words, it is all whether the time investment is worth it -- hard to say "in the abstract" how it will go. For instance, very bad LLM patches (even if audited by a human), may not be worth it; but good patches that are close to what a kernel developer would have submitted would be more successful.

In addition, please note that, submitters of code generated by tools are expected to understand the code as if they had written it themselves. That is, even if a tool generates perfect code, the submitter should still be able to answer questions about it etc.

Finally, maintainers have discretion in how to handle contributions like this, so you will definitely want to find someone that is open to this (and I thank you for being open about your system too!).

For the official kernel guidelines on how to handle generated content (including LLM-assisted content etc.), please see:

  • https://docs.kernel.org/process/generated-content.html
  • https://docs.kernel.org/process/coding-assistants.html

Therefore, we are looking for:

Yeah, that is usually the tricky bit... :)

One thing you can do is announce it clearly, e.g. asking here in Zulip and/or in the mailing list what you need, with a good title like "Looking for C driver for testing LLM-based translation tool" or something like that. And then perhaps a place like LWN picks it up and someone contacts you.

Conferences are also another place to find people and potential ideas, but it is time consuming, of course.

The easiest, in fact, may be to research online for existing out-of-tree drivers that are not upstream. The code quality may not be great, though.

But that could be a selling point: if your system can not just translate, but also create a proper Rust driver of greater quality, that could be quite interesting! Of course, please be careful with licensing, attribution, etc. -- those may be hard/open questions...

Another idea is just doing it with an existing Rust driver, to be honest -- e.g. take one of the drivers that have already been human-written in Rust upstream, and that have a C version, and compare the result of your tool vs. the normally-written driver. That may be a good first step to establish what your tool can do, so that then other maintainers may be interested (I bet you already do comparisons in your research, of course, but since you are asking for a "real case", then I guess perhaps you did not do it with an upstream one).

However, we fully intend to open-source the toolchain alongside our research publication.

That is great, thanks! Open source tools usually are a good way to get maintainers' attention, so that may help.

I hope all this helps somehow, and good luck!

We are seeing quite a few patches sent to the list from your address -- are those coming out of this system?

If so, shouldn't the patches be marked as LLM-assisted etc.?

By the way, please also review https://docs.kernel.org/process/researcher-guidelines.html

Thanks!

Thanks for flagging this. We have carefully reviewed the relevant documents (generated-content, coding-assistants, and researcher-guidelines). We apologize for the initial omission and will follow the requirements to modify and resend the patches we have already submitted with the proper disclosures.
You're welcome, and thanks again for following the guidelines :)
